coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots with high photocatalytic reactivity by tailoring heteroatom doping

coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant by carbon-based materials is still a challenge. Herein, xylose-derived carbon dots (X-CDs) and chitosan-derived CDs (C-CDs) were synthesized by heteroatoms-doping strategy. Although there is almost no difference in fluorescence emission behaviors, the two types of CDs demonstrated different advantages in photocatalysis and peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Comparative research revealed that the X-CDs with doping of heteroatom S was superior in the separation of electron-hole pairs, resulting in a higher catalytic performance, while the S, N co-doped C-CDs can only exhibit high photocatalytic reactivity when they were coupled with PMS. The underlying reason is that the N-related functional groups with strong electron-donating property weakened the electron-trapping capacity of S-related energy level, but surface state resulting from this doping structures were conducive to promoting photo-generated electron transfer from C-CDs to PMS and played the primary role in organic oxidation. Thanks to the doping effect, both the X-CDs and C-CDs/PMS system displayed high photocatalytic performance for methylene blue removal under sunlight irradiation, showing almost 100% degradation efficiency in a 30 min period. The present study provides a valuable insight for the synthesis of CDs-based catalysts but also establishes a very promising catalytic oxidation system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL emission spectra of (a) X-CDs and (b) C-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, showing similar excitation-dependent emission behavior. Demonstration of self-absorption behavior for (c) X-CDs and (d) C-CDs by characterizing absorption and excitation spectra. Plot of (Ahʋ)2 vs. photon energy calculated from diffuse reflectance spectra of (e) X-CDs and (f) C-CDs, confirming better photosensitiveness of X-CDs than C-CDs under sunlight.

TEM images and histograms of the corresponding particle size distribution of (a) X-CDs and (b) C-CDs. Raman spectral features of (c) X-CDs and (d) C-CDs, the D band at around 1355 cm−1 and the G band at around 1560 cm−1.

The expanded XPS of C 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p spectra of (a-c) X-CDs and (d-f) C-CDs, and (g) distinct high resolution XPS of N 1 s spectrum of C-CDs. The deconvolution results demonstrate the same C-, O-, and S-related chemical species within X-CDs, and C-CDs, while N-containing moieties were also detected from C-CDs because of nitrogen self-doping.

Photocatalytic degradation of MB in the absence and presence of (a) X-CDs and (b) C-CDs in the darkness or sunlight, respectively, demonstrating that the X-CDs are superior in photocatalysis. For (c) X-CDs and (d) C-CDs, effects of different reactive species (h+, •OH and O2 •−) on the photodegradation efficiency of MB in the presence of scavengers ([MB] = 30 mg/L, [CDs] = 1 g/L, [Na2C2O4] = 100 mM, [t-BA] = 250 mM, [NBD-Cl] = 200 μM).

(a) PNP degradation in presence of X-CDs. (b) Repetitive photodegradation of MB by X-CDs over time. Effects of (c) pH values and (d) different inorganic anions on MB degradation by X-CDs under sunlight irradiation ([MB] = 30 mg/L, [X-CDs] = 1 g/L, [NaHCO3] = [Na2CO3] = [Na2SO4] = [NaNO3] = [NaCl] = 250 mM).
Catalytic degradation of MB by (a) X-CDs/PMS system and (b) C-CDs/PMS system under different conditions, respectively. ([MB] = 30 mg/L, [CDs] = 30 mg/L, [PMS] = 1 mM).

Effects of various quenching agents on decomposition of MB by (a) X-CDs/PMS system and (b) C-CDs/PMS system under sunlight irradiation ([MB] = 30 mg/L, [CDs] = 30 mg/L, [PMS] = 1 mM, [MA] = [t-BA] = [KI] = 500 mM, [SN] = [Na2C2O4] = 50 mM, [FAA] = [TEMP] = 100 mM, [NBD-Cl] = 200 μM).

Effect of the initial pH on MB degradation by (a) X-CDs/PMS system and (b) C-CDs/PMS system under sunlight irradiation. (c-d) Changes in PL intensity of CDs and CDs/PMS systems under 360 nm excitation at different pH values.

(a) PNP degradation in presence of C-CDs/PMS. (b) Repetitive photodegradation of MB by C-CDs/PMS system over time. (c) Degradation efficiency of MB by C-CDs/PMS system in the presence of inorganic anions ([MB] = 30 mg/L, [C-CDs] = 30 mg/L, [PMS] = 1 mM, [NaHCO3] = [Na2CO3] = [Na2SO4] = [NaNO3] = [NaCl] = 20 mM). (d) Effects of the high concentration of inorganic anions (250 mM) on MB degradation by C-CDs/PMS system.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots with high photocatalytic reactivity by tailoring heteroatom doping

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant by carbon-based materials is still a challenge. Herein, xylose-derived carbon dots (X-CDs) and chitosan-derived CDs (C-CDs) were synthesized by heteroatoms-doping strategy. Although there is almost no difference in fluorescence emission behaviors, the two types of CDs demonstrated different advantages in photocatalysis and peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Comparative research revealed that the X-CDs with doping of heteroatom S was superior in the separation of electron-hole pairs, resulting in a higher catalytic performance, while the S, N co-doped C-CDs can only exhibit high photocatalytic reactivity when they were coupled with PMS. The underlying reason is that the N-related functional groups with strong electron-donating property weakened the electron-trapping capacity of S-related energy level, but surface state resulting from this doping structures were conducive to promoting photo-generated electron transfer from C-CDs to PMS and played the primary role in organic oxidation. Thanks to the doping effect, both the X-CDs and C-CDs/PMS system displayed high photocatalytic performance for methylene blue removal under sunlight irradiation, showing almost 100% degradation efficiency in a 30 min period. The present study provides a valuable insight for the synthesis of CDs-based catalysts but also establishes a very promising catalytic oxidation system.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past years, noble metal nanoparticles and metal oxides, with excellent photo-sensitivity and high activity, have played a pivotal role in photocatalytic field. Despite this, the high recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs, lack of visible light absorption, serious aggregation induced by high surface energy as well as expensive cost still build a barrier for their practical application [1,2]. Carbon-based materials (eg, activated carbon, graphene et al.), thanks to their excellent chemical and thermal stability, are highly appropriate for combining with metal materials to design composites as photocatalysts. Besides providing anchoring sites for metal semiconductors, the integration of carbon-based materials is also beneficial for absorbing pollutants to accelerate the degradation rate [3,4]. More importantly, carbon-based component in composites can serve as an electron acceptor/donor to suppress the recombination of photo-excited electron-hole pairs, thus being conducive to generating reactive species that are responsible for pollutant degradation [5,6]. Nevertheless, establishing an effective interface structure for electron transfer is complicated and difficult to control; also, the unavoidable leakage of metal-related component caused by chemical inertness of carbon is still a pivotal problem. Therefore, there remains a need to develop metal-free carbon-based materials with high efficiency as environmentally friendly catalysts. To improve the catalytic performance of pristine carbon-based materials, several strategies have been successfully applied, including structure defect engineering, heteroatom doping, surface functionalization and so on [7,8]. On the other hand, the addition of oxidants can provide a solution to the application bottlenecks of carbon-based materials without or with poor photocatalytic activity. It has been reported that carbon-based materials have emerged as green catalysts for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation due to their efficient active sites induced by diversified structure defects and heteroatoms-containing groups [9,10]. However, synthesis and functionalization of traditional carbon-based materials always require concentrated acids, high temperature and complex post treatment steps, which blocks their practical industrial applications.

As a relatively new class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated their potential as a suitable quantum-sized carbocatalyst or PMS activators by taking advantages of small size effect, better photo-response and excellent charge carrier migration [11,12]. In recent years, extensive researches have proved that CDs with surface functionalization are able to be characterized by excellent photocatalytic activity. For instance, Jiang et al. synthesized P- and S-doped CDs as metal-free photocatalysts for visible-light-regulated reversible addition fragmentation chain-transfer polymerization [13]. Han et al. reported carboxyl-functionalized CDs as efficient visible light photocatalyst for aerobic oxygenation of alkyl benzenes [14]. Duan et al. engineered the surface chemical state of CDs by selectively screening ketonic groups, doing so significantly enhanced the activity of CDs for PMS activation [15]. However, the contribution of their surface chemical structure or even electron transition behavior to the photocatalysis capacity of CDs is still ambiguous, because, for varieties of CDs, there are great differences in precursors, synthetic methods and dopants [16,17]. As such, it is necessary to deeply research the underlying reason of CDs photocatalysis that is imparted by the multiple surface states. More specifically, surface states within CDs are commonly dominated by heteroatom doping, thus, elucidating the correlations between photocatalysis and different heteroatom doping effects is also a basic task. Another focus of attention is the use of cost-effective carbonaceous precursor for CDs synthesis. In this regard, biomass is advantageous in terms of low cost, widespread distribution and abundant resources, which will soundly promote the progress not only in the value-added utilization of biomass waste, but also in the development of photocatalytic CDs.

In this research, xylose, which is derived from waste biomass, was employed as carbon source to synthesize xylose-derived CDs (X-CDs) with photocatalytic activity in the presence of thionyl chloride as dopant. Simultaneously, the chitosan, which can provide an endogenous N-doping, was also selected as biomass-based carbonaceous precursor to synthesize chitosan-derived CDs (C-CDs), using thionyl chloride as identical extra dopant. These two types of CDs were characterized by the almost same photoluminescence (PL) behaviors, but their photocatalytic performance was greatly different. Through detailed characterization on optical properties, morphology, and chemical composition, a possible mechanism on photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CDs was proposed. In addition, the N-doped CDs, which had lower photocatalytic activity but better fluorescence efficiency, outperformed X-CDs in PMS activation, owing to the facile generation of reactive species in the presence of PMS under sunlight. Further investigation focused on the PMS activation mechanism was also performed for better understanding the relationship between the chemical composition of CDs and their PMS activation efficiency. Benefiting from excellent photocatalytic activity resulting from both pristine X-CDs and C-CDs/PMS coupling system, as well as their high anti-interference performance in methylene blue (MB) degradation (e.g. pH variation and inorganic anions), this work demonstrates a simple method for efficient utilization of biomass-based CDs in practical pollutant treatment.

X-CDs was synthesized in a typical route. 0.2 g of xylose and 200 μL thionyl chloride were dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to the room temperature completely, the reactants were purified via a 0.22 μm filter membrane and heated at 95 °C with a duration of 30 min to get rid of possible existed volatile gas in the CDs solution. Then the resulting mixtures were dialyzed (100 Da of cut off molecular weight) for 48 h to remove the excess precursor and possible side products. For the preparation of C-CDs, 10 mL of diluted acetic acid solution (2% w/w) was used as solvent to dissolve chitosan, the synthesis procedure was otherwise the same as the preparation of X-CDs.
Details related to other experiments were presented in Supplementary Material.

The emission spectra of the synthesized CDs were investigated, as shown in Fig. 1 a and b. Clearly, both the X-CDs and C-CDs exhibited typical excitation-dependent PL behaviors as the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 440 nm, and the PL intensity increased initially and decreased later within the excitation wavelength range. Specifically, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the two different CDs samples were almost same (360 and 450 nm), thus demonstrating their identical PL emission sites. As another proof, the emission peaks as a function of excitation wavelengths that was determined from X-CDs and C-CDs samples also showed a consistent variation (Figure S1). In general, the excitation-dependent luminescence behavior is a common feature of fluorescent CDs synthesized via heteroatom doping strategy, which is likely attributed to numerous energy levels within their structures [18]. Since the almost same emission behavior can be observed from the two CDs samples, it is reasonable to deduce that the thionyl chloride played a key role in generating defect states during the formation of both X-CDs and C-CDs. The resulted defect states involving radiative recombination could be highly similar regardless of the distinction in heteroatom-related chemical species [19]. In addition, benefiting from the broad excitation band of these CDs, the light utilization and photoelectric conversion efficiency could be improved when they are used as photocatalysts. Besides, the PL emission spectra of CDs synthesized from xylose or chitosan alone as the precursor were also measured (Fig. S2). In comparison to the CDs without dopants, doping thionyl chloride resulted in the shift of maximum emission peak from 440 to 456 nm for X-CDs, and from 404 to 459 nm for C-CDs. The redshift phenomenon indicates that doping effect resulted from thionyl chloride created the same new defect states in X-CDs and C-CDs. More specifically, it is reasonable to assume that the incorporation of S-related groups induced an intermediate energy level under the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of defect states from n-π* transitions, this emerging electron transition channel could be mainly responsible for the PL emission [20].

In the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of X-CDs, there was only one predominant band peaked at 310 nm, which was commonly induced by the n-π* transition of CO moieties [21], while for the C-CDs, the two absorption peaked at 268 and 300 nm were correspond to the π-π* transition of CC bonds of aromatic rings and n-π* transition of CO and/or CN bonds, respectively (Fig. 1c and d). Moreover, the PL excitation spectra of the two types of CDs obtained at optimal emissions demonstrated similar profile with a peak at around 355 nm. Remarkably, the absorption band located at 300 nm was overlapped with excitation spectrum for the synthesized CDs samples, thus confirming that the surface states mainly induced by functionalization contribute greatly to the PL emission of both X-CDs and C-CDs. For X-CDs, the tailed absorption band from UV-light region to visible-light region revealed the significant self-absorption behavior of these CDs. Generally, the weak self-absorption facilitates the efficient electronic transition and thus give rise to the enhanced luminescence efficiency, while the increased self-absorption could contribute to the valid separation of electrons (e−) and holes (h+), which is conducive to generating numerous reactive species during photocatalytic process [22]. Diffuse reflectance spectra measurement was also carried out to plot relationship between (Ahʋ)2 and photon energy. As displayed in Fig. 1e-f, the calculated band gap energies of X-CDs and C-CDs were 2.03 and 2.21 eV, respectively, confirming the visible light response capability of CDs. Therefore, the as-prepared CDs can be considered as direct semiconductors with narrow band gap that is well-suited for homogeneous photocatalytic degradation reactions. Further, the fluorescence lifetime of each CDs was monitored for comparison, as shown in Fig. S3. The obtained lifetime decay curves were well fitted by a double-exponential function, revealing two components of fluorescence decay, the former decay component (τ1) was generally induced by electronic transitions within the conjugated sp2 domains, while the later decay component (τ2) was generated from defect states that were commonly induced by heteroatom doping effect [23]. The average lifetimes of X-CDs and C-CDs were also calculated as 0.87 and 0.43 ns. Furthermore, it can be found that the fluorescence decay was mainly from τ2 component because its weight was as high as 99.98%, evidencing that the defect states took the dominant place in controlling PL behavior. Moreover, the PL quantum yield (QY) was determined as 3.40% for X-CDs and 17.21% for C-CDs in the reference to quinine sulfate (QY = 54%). Due to the endogenous N-doping for C-CDs, the electron density was more likely to be increased, and hereby facilitated the e− transition and ultimately contributed to the improvement of QY [24]. According to previous research [25], we also evaluated the radiative transition rate (K R) and non-radiative rate (K NR) using the known fluorescence lifetime and QY, by calculation based on the formulas

, the ratio of K R to K NR for X-CDs and C-CDs were determined as 0.035 and 0.208, respectively. By comparison, it is reasonable to suppose that the efficient recombination of electron-hole pairs was more readily to occur within excited C-CDs.

All the synthesized CDs showed decentralized nanoparticles in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2 a and b). The as-prepared X-CDs were very tiny in size with an average diameter of 1.69 ± 0.45 nm, and the average diameter of C-CDs was calculated to be 2.84 ± 0.55 nm, which was slightly larger than that of X-CDs. In view of their almost same particle size distribution and excitation-dependent emission performances, the multiple surface defect states involving radiative and non-radiative transitions rather than the quantum size effect should be responsible for PL emissions, which in turn reveals that doping heteroatoms was more likely to have strong effect on the photocatalytic properties of CDs [20]. The Raman spectra showed a sp2-hybrid G-band at 1560 cm−1 and a defect-induced d-band at 1355 cm−1 (Fig. 2c-d). The proportion of sp3 and sp2 carbon was determined as 2.87 and 1.90 for X-CDs and C-CDs by calculating the intensity ratio of ID/IG, respectively, indicating a relatively high content of disordered carbon domains in the sample probably generated by heteroatom-doping, and thus more surface defects induced.

The surface functional groups of photocatalytic CDs were detected by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. As presented in Fig. S4, the dull adsorptions peak at ~ 3388 cm−1 was related to O H stretching vibration, and the broad band with relative high intensity at about 3030 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of = C H, indicating that the as-prepared X-CDs and C-CDs could have large conjugated degree [26]. Besides, common stretching vibration peaks of –COOH (1404 cm−1), CO (1711 cm−1) and CC (1631 cm−1) can be also observed from all samples. Considering that the thionyl chloride participated in the formation of each photocatalytic CDs sample, it was reasonable to suppose that the distinct absorption bands appeared at 2450, 1185 and 1060 cm−1 were respectively induced by the stretching vibration of S H, C S and CS functionalities [27,28]. Moreover, the vibrational peaks at around 500–900 cm−1 were more likely to be originated from C − Cl bond in chlorinated aliphatic hydrocarbons [29]. As mentioned above, both the X-CDs and C-CDs were functionalized with S- and Cl-related chemical groups, and similar defect states could be formed within conjugated structures. As known, S has a lower electronegativity than N and O, while Cl is characterized by relatively high electronegativity. Based on this, we deduced that the co-existence of S-, and Cl-related groups is prone to change the e− transition channels within the sp2-domains of CDs. Moreover, it should be mentioned that these abundant hydrophilic groups attached on the surfaces of CDs are advantageous for their dispersion in aqueous solution and may offer plentiful active sites for physical absorption and chemical reaction during the photodegradation of pollutes.

The element composition and valence state of synthesized CDs were further investigated by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. As illustrated in Fig. S5a, from the survey spectrum of X-CDs, the characteristic peak of C 1 s (284.9 eV), O 1 s (531.5 eV), S 2p (168.6 eV), Cl 2p (198.7 eV), and S 2 s (229.2 eV) were detectable, demonstrating their elemental composition of C (58.11%, atomic percentage), O (34.75%, atomic percentage), S (6.97%, atomic percentage) and Cl (0.16%, atomic percentage). Differing from the X-CDs, as seen in Figure S5b, the XPS information obtained from C-CDs showed distinct signal of N 1 s (401.3 eV), illustrating the incorporation of N element (8.64%) within CDs in addition to the composition of C (47.46%), O (36.00%), S (7.64%) and Cl (0.26%). As presented in Fig. 3 , the high resolution XPS of C 1 s spectrum exhibited four characteristic peaks at 284.6, 285.5, 286.7 and 288.6 eV, representing C C/CC, C S, C − Cl/C O and O − CO, respectively [30,31]. Particularly for the C-CDs, we believed that the C N groups (285.5 eV) could be also contained within their structure due to the endogenous N-doping from chitosan. From the deconvolution of O 1 s and S 2p bands, it can be seen that there was almost no difference in either O-related species or S-related species between these CDs samples. Specifically, the O 1 s spectra validate the existence of CO (531.3 eV), O − CO (532.3 eV) and C − OH/C O − C (533.5 eV), respectively [32]. Additionally, the S 2p spectrum can be deconvoluted into two primary peaks at 164.0 and 168.7 eV, suggesting that the doped S element existed in two different forms. The former peaks revealed two chemical species with binding energy at 163.2 and 164.4 eV, representing the reported 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the - C S - covalent bond of the thiophene-S, owing to their spin–orbit couplings. The latter peak can be resolved into three different components, arising from different –C–SOx– (x = 2, 3, 4) species with binding energy at 167.8, 168.6 and 169.5 eV [33]. In particular, the high-resolution N 1 s band obtained from the C-CDs showed three peaks with bonding energy at 399.3, 400.3 and 401.3 eV, revealing the formation of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively, further confirming that the C-CDs were also doped with N element in addition to the incorporation of S and Cl elements [34]. Based on the first-principles calculations proposed by Yan et al., a great number of functional groups, as C − OH and C O − C, contributed to level-split within the n-π* gap and thus resulted in the excitation-dependent PL behaviors [35]. Moreover, these defected levels with relative narrow band gap exhibited better response to excited light, which favors the natural light utilization in terms of photocatalysis.

According to the discussion mentioned above, it can be concluded that the n-π* transition is dominant in the electronic transition because of the abundant O-related groups on the CDs surfaces. Besides, a new energy level resulted from S-related groups lies above ground state, which is capable of trapping photo-excited e− from ground state (n orbitals) and relaxed e− from excited state (π* orbitals). Based on this, the intermediate energy level could delay the recombination of electron-hole pairs in ground state. Although the C-CDs was doped with endogenous N successfully, it mainly existed in the form of graphitic N within the carbogenic cores, this functionalization could contribute to improve QY of CDs by enhancing their surface electron density but had a little effect on the excitation-dependent emission behaviors [36,37].

Several previous researches have pointed out that understanding the adsorption performance of catalysts is necessary to interpret their photoactivity, thus, preliminary studies of MB adsorption by X-CDs and C-CDs were performed in dark environment within 1 h. Specifically, before adsorption experiments, both two types of CDs solution went through heating at 90 °C for 20 min to get rid of the possible effect of absorbed thionyl chloride. As seen in Fig. S6, the determined adsorption kinetics illustrate that the X-CDs or C-CDs had maximum MB absorption after 25–35 min, confirming that the suspension basically reached the balance of adsorption and desorption after 30 min. The equilibrium conditions indicated that the further reduction of MB concentration under sunlight irradiation can be attributed to the photodegradation by CDs. Notably, the C-CDs showed slightly higher MB absorption, the underlying reason could be related to the electrostatic interaction between deprotonated amino groups on C-CDs surfaces and MB molecules in alkaline condition.

To examine the photocatalytic activities of the prepared CDs, the photodegradation of organic dyes by the two CDs samples were performed, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. Upon illuminating with sunlight, both the two types of CDs showed photocatalytic degradation toward MB. In specific, the photocatalytic degradation of MB by X-CDs was around 92.06% after 2.5 min irradiation. In contrast, the C-CDs exhibited low photocatalytic efficiency with a removal rate of only 29.33% by prolonging the irradiation time to 30 min, indicating their inferior photocatalytic performance compared with the X-CDs. Since the total organic carbon (TOC) is more representative and useful for evaluating the degradation efficiencies rather than the degradation rates, the TOC changes were inspected before and after photocatalytic tests. The TOC removal efficiency over X-CDs was 93.48%, which ran up to 3.27 times than that of C-CDs (28.57%), indicating that the former can more effectively decompose MB molecules into small inorganic molecules/ions (Fig. S7). Besides, to confirm the critical role of CDs samples in the photodegradation of MB, several control experiments under various conditions were also performed (Figs. S8 and S9). The self-decomposition of MB in the sunlight can be hardly occurred without adding CDs samples. Even in the presence of thionyl chloride, the degradation of MB only reached 10.12% after 30 min irradiation, which ruled out the possible photodegradation of MB by thionyl chloride. Furthermore, the CDs synthesized without using thionyl chloride as dopant can hardly degrade MB under sunlight, and the catalytic efficiencies have almost no changes even adding thionyl chloride into these catalytic systems. Therefore, it is reasonable to deduce that the surface state induced by S-containing groups contributed greatly to the photocatalysis of X-CDs.

Further, the special roles of reactive species in photocatalytic reaction were probed to confirm the plausible degradation mechanism of the MB (Fig. 4c and d). The sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), tertiary butyl alcohol (t-BA) and 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzofurazan (NBD-Cl) were generally selected as the scavengers for the trap of h+, hydroxyl radical (•OH) and superoxide (O2 •−), respectively [38,39]. The quantitative analysis of O2 •− confirmed its primary role in the first 5 min (Fig. S10). Herein, the contribution of different reactive species to MB degradation was evaluated (Table S1), and all scavengers exhibited an inhibitory effect on MB degradation, demonstrating that the CDs in sunlight irradiation were prone to induce the generation of h+, •OH and O2 •− and thus contributed greatly to photocatalysis. We speculate that the sharply degradation of MB within the first 2.5 min is highly related to the dissolved O2, because O2 •− can be easily formed by the combination of electrons with O2 adsorbed on the surfaces of X-CDs. Thus, a N2 purging experiment was performed to confirm the possible role of dissolved O2 in aqueous solution on the MB degradation by X-CDs. As seen in Fig. S11, MB degradation was inhibited significantly under the condition of N2 purge, proving that O2 as the acceptor of photoexcited e− is important to the removal of MB. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis was further performed to identify the possible free radicals generated from X-CDs in degradation process (Fig. S12). Specifically, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was used as trapping agent for detecting •OH in ultrapure water and O2 •− in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), respectively. As seen, the typical 1:2:2:1 and 1:1:1:1 quadruplex signals corresponded to DMPO-•OH and DMPO-O2 •−, suggesting that X-CDs were favorable for generation of a large amount of •OH and O2 •−, and thus contributed to the MB degradation [40]. On the basis of these aforementioned results, a possible photoreaction mechanism for MB photodegradation using X-CDs was proposed. Because S element has lower electronegativity than O or N element, doping heteroatom S could create a new level which lies above ground state, and subsequently has large effect on the electron transition behavior of CDs.Therefore, for the S-doped X-CDs, most of photoexcited e− can be easily trapped by the S-related energy level, resulting in great amount of h+ located in the ground state, that is, the recombination of photoelectron-hole pairs was inhibited seriously by doping heteroatom S [41]. The photo-excited e− from X-CDs will reduce the adsorbed oxidants (usually O2) to O2 •−, while h+ can be donated to the hydroxyl groups and water to generate •OH or react with MB directly [42]. Taken together, these reactive species (h+, •OH and O2 •−) participated in the photodegradation of MB. In the light of the above results, we deduced that the inferior photocatalysis of C-CDs is related to the high graphitic nitrogen content arising from the endogenic N doping by chitosan, because this structure is conducive to donating excess electrons into the unoccupied π* orbitals and thus facilitating a high yield of radiative recombination [43,44]. To illustrate the issue that photocatalysis of X-CDs was free from the effect of dye sensitization, p-nitrophenol (PNP) degradation using X-CDs was also investigated and the result was shown in Fig. 5 a, as seen, the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak of PNP decreased with the catalytic time increasing, indicating that the X-CDs displayed excellent photocatalysis. Besides, recycling study was also carried out to check the stability and durability of X-CDs as photocatalyst. Photodegradation efficiency of X-CDs on MB showed a loss of 20.57% after the use of five cycles, but still maintained around 80% (Fig. 5b).

Considering the pH variation of water pollutants and the existence of inorganic anions in practical application, the relevant experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 5c, as the pH value increased from 5 to 9, the MB degradation efficiency appeared to be unaltered, showing a high removal rate above 96.00% in a 30 min period. In addition, Fig. 5d showed that inorganic anions (HCO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and Cl−) with high concentrations inhibited MB degradation at varying levels. Particularly, the addition of HCO3 − and CO3 2− exerted the highest inhibition effect, which was related to the reaction of HCO3 − or CO3 2− with •OH to produce carbonate radicals (CO3 •−) with a lower redox potential (Eq. (1) and (2)) [45]. In a nutshell, X-CDs can maintain great photocatalytic activity in a wide range of pH values and high concentration of inorganic anions, thus, X-CDs can be employed for pollutants degradation in practical environment.
(1) HCO3 − + •OH → CO3 •− + H2O
(2) CO3 2− + •OH → CO3 •− + OH

To further expand the application of CDs in pollutant degradation, the potential effect of CDs on PMS activation, as well as the intrinsic mechanism were also studied. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the MB degradation using CDs alone and CDs/PMS coupling system was investigated in both dark and sunlight condition. As seen, PMS alone can hardly degrade MB, but the catalytic activity resulting from PMS was enhanced remarkably in the presence of X-CDs or C-CDs. Specifically, the degradation rate of MB in the X-CDs/PMS system reached 43.72% after 30 min irradiation, while, within the same irradiation period, the C-CDs/PMS system demonstrated to be more effective in the activation of PMS, showing degradation rate of 98.04%. Therefore, it can be seen that using X-CDs and C-CDs respectively to construct PMS-based coupling system demonstrated opposite photocatalytic capacity compared with themselves. Additionally, the effect of CDs/PMS system on MB removal was also investigated in dark condition, and the results revealed that 44.47% MB removal can be resulted from C-CDs/PMS system, which was much higher than that resulted from X-CDs/PMS system (21.95%). The effect of CDs/PMS system on the mineralization of MB was also evaluated by inspecting the TOC changes. As shown in Fig. S13, the TOC removal rate was in great line with the corresponding degradation ratio, indicating the complete decomposition of MB molecule during catalytic process. Based on above analysis, the as-prepared C-CDs outperformed X-CDs in activating PMS, the probable reason for this difference was closely related to the high electron transfer efficiency of N-doped C-CDs.

The influence of CDs dosage on MB degradation was evaluated to optimize the CDs dosage for PMS activation. As displayed in Fig. S14. it is clear that the efficiency of X-CDs/PMS degradation of MB was almost independent on the X-CDs concentration ranging from 15 to 60 mg/L, we hypothesize that the competitive reaction between X-CDs/PMS system and X-CDs themselves was involved due to the high photocatalytic activity of X-CDs. In contrast, the degradation efficiencies of MB using C-CDs/PMS system can be significantly boosted by increasing the C-CDs concentration from 15 to 30 mg/L, but further increasing C-CD dosage can hardly affect the MB removal, which is likely due to the fact that the reactive species concentration was basic saturated when the dosage of C-CDs was reached a suitable level. Thus, to avoid the excessive physical adsorption or light screening during the photocatalysis, the optimal CDs dosage used for the subsequent investigations was chosen as 30 mg/L.

In order to exclude the possible reactions between quenching agents and PMS, MB degradation by PMS were studied in absence of CDs. As shown in Fig. S15, the addition of quenching agents played no significant inhibiting or promoting role in MB degradation, ruling out the effect of quenching agents on PMS activation. the relevant radical quenching experiments were further carried out to identify the reactive species accounting for MB removal by CDs/PMS systems (Fig. 7 , Fig. S16 and Table S2). After adding methanol (MA), t-BA and NBD-Cl into the X-CDs/PMS system under irradiation, the negligible changes in degradation efficiency manifested that sulfate radical (SO4 •−), •OH and O2 •− free in suspension had limited effects on MB removal. The concentration of fluorescent NBD-Cl product also demonstrated that only a small amount of O2 •− was generated. Besides, it was found that the degradation efficiency was reduced after the addition of potassium iodide (KI), because the surface-bond SO4 •− can be quenched rapidly by KI. In addition, silver nitrate (SN) and Na2C2O4, as sacrificial agents for e− and h+, resulted in an inhibiting effect on MB degradation. As confirmed by analysis on optical performances of X-CDs, the relatively low PL efficiency was soundly attributed to a fact that photo-generated electrons can be easily trapped by multiple defect states induced by O- and S-related groups. Thus, for the X-CDs/PMS coupling system, we deduce that the electron donation of X-CDs for PMS activation was inferior, and more importantly, the active sites on X-CDs surfaces with catalytic behavior were also disrupted by PMS. As such, reactive species generating from X-CDs suffered a low concentration level, resulting in a low degradation efficiency of MB. Furthermore, furfuryl alcohol (FFA) or 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were added into the X-CDs/PMS system to identify the existence of singlet oxygen (1O2), and it was observed that the degradation efficiency of MB declined. Since 1O2 is usually regarded as the typical reactive oxygen species for non-radical process, it can be ascertained that non-radical pathway also participated in X-CDs/PMS degradation of MB under sunlight irradiation. As confirmed by previous research, nano-carbon material with surface-contained CO groups can accelerate 1O2 production, which highly associated with non-radical PMS activation [46,47]. In our case, X-CDs with surface-contained abundant CO groups can activate PMS and mediate the non-photochemical production of 1O2 through Eq. (3), especially under weak alkaline condition due to MB.
(3) HSO5 − + SO5 2− → HSO4 − + SO4 2− + 1O2

For the C-CDs/PMS system, the inhibiting effects of scavengers on MB removal were significant under sunlight. Although the formation of O2 •− was relatively higher than that of X-CDs/PMS system, it can be inferred that the SO4 •− and •OH were the primary reactive species in C-CDs/PMS system. Besides, the additions of KI, SN and Na2C2O4 reduced the removal rate, indicating that SO4 •− was mostly concentrated on the surfaces of C-CDs, and the h+ also participated in the MB removal. It was speculated that transferred e− could be the root cause of PMS activation and production of reactive species (SO4 •−, •OH and O2 •−). Moreover, the 1O2 was also confirmed to be generated in the catalytic system by using either FFA or TEMP as scavenger. Therefore, the radical pathway was identified as the protagonist for photocatalytic degradation of MB by C-CDs/PMS system. The involved reactive species in the C-CDs/PMS system under sunlight irradiation were also detected by EPR. Spin-trapping reagents of DMPO were used to detect SO4 •− and •OH, and O2 •− respectively; also, TEMP was used to explore 1O2. As shown in Fig. S17, it is known that DMPO-SO4 •− adducts were unstable and could easily convert to 5,5-dimethyl-2-oxo-pyrroline-1-oxyl and DMPO-•OH adducts, the intense signal representing •OH was obtained and signal of SO4 •− was weak to be isolated indistinctly in EPR spectrum [48]. Thus, in order to validate the existence of SO4 •−, 5-tert-butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (BMPO) was employed. A characteristic seven-line spectra can be observed, assigning to 5-tert-Butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-2-oxo-pyrroline-1-oxyl (BMPOX) which might come from the reaction of BMPO with SO4 •− radicals [49]. Besides, there was unsharp peak intensity of O2 •− detected, illustrating that there was almost no reaction pathway to generate O2 •− by C-CDs/PMS system in degradation process. Moreover, the EPR spectra showed characteristic signals relating to TEMP-1O2, which confirms the participation of 1O2 in the C-CDs/PMS system for MB degradation [50]. Therefore, SO4 •−, •OH, and 1O2 were involved in the degradation process, which is beneficial for improving the MB removal. Besides, the π-π* interaction between the conjugated structure of CDs and the benzene ring of MB can also enhance the photocatalytic activity [51].

According to above-mentioned analysis, the higher PMS activation capacity of C-CDs could be understood in part by efficient e− transfer process. In specific, it has been confirmed that the C-CDs functionalized with abundant N-containing groups was due to the in situ doping from chitosan. This nitrogen doping configurations, especially abundant graphitic N, can largely increase the electron density of surface defect states, and thus further facilitated the reaction between PMS and C-CDs [52]. As another important proof for the high consumption of photo-generated e− in C-CD/PMS system, the decrease in PL intensity of C-CDs was much larger than that of X-CDs when they were coupled with PMS (Fig. S18). PMS can be well activated by C-CDs to form SO4 •− and •OH through Eqn 4, 5 and 6Eq. (4)-(6) [53]. On the other hand, the pyrrolic N present in carbonaceous materials was previously found to be the active site for adsorption of organic contaminants, which is beneficial to subsequent photocatalytic effect on MB [54].
(4) HSO5 − + hv → SO4 •− + •OH
(5) HSO5 − + e− → SO4 •− + OH−
(6) SO4 •− + OH− → SO4 2− + •OH

The enhancement of MB removal by C-CDs/PMS system under irradiation can be attributed to the production of e− and h+ from C-CDs, the C-CDs/PMS system also showed great catalytic activity toward MB in dark. The reactive species that could be responsible for MB removal in the dark were also identified through radical quenching experiments (Fig. S19a-b) and summarized in Table S3. Because the slight changes in the degradation efficiency can be observed after the addition of MA, t-BA, and NBD-Cl, the role of degradation effect by SO4 •−, •OH and O2 •− free in suspension was eliminated. The existence of surface-bounded SO4 •− was also proved by 7.87% reduction in MB degradation ratio because significant inhibition can be imparted by KI. Since the inhibition of SN and Na2C2O4 on MB degradation was weakened compared with that under sunlight irradiation, it was considered that e− transfer to PMS were limited seriously due to the unexcited C-CDs. In addition, FFA or TEMP suppressed the MB removal sharply, ascertaining that non-radical pathway played a dominant role in the degradation of MB by C-CDs/PMS system in dark condition. Moreover, three peaks in EPR spectra with approximate intensity relating to TEMP-1O2 spin adducts were observed when testing in dark environment, and the intensity was increased with the addition of C-CDs. Such phenomenon demonstrated that the generation of 1O2 mainly depended on the activation of C-CDs rather than merely relying on the self-decomposition of PMS (Fig. S19c) [55]. As same as X-CDs, C-CDs surfaces was also attached CO groups that may act as reactive sites, thus, PMS can be activated and decomposed into 1O2 through the non-radical pathway [46,47].

Fig. 8 a and b shows the effect of initial pH on MB degradation by CDs/PMS system under sunlight irradiation. It was found that the efficiency of MB degradation by CDs/PMS systems were significantly improved as the suspension pH was above 7. The reason for the increased degradation activity may be the promotion of non-radical pathway in non-acid conditions. The inferior photocatalysis in acid condition could be attributed to the change of PMS, because H-bond between H+ and O O group of HSO5 − could be formed easily under acidic condition, which hinder the interaction between HSO5 − and CDs surface [56]. Normally, PMS activation capacity of CDs in relation to e− transfer can be revealed by investigating the variation of PL intensity, that is, the decreased PL intensity means the low recombination of carriers or high electron consumption. As shown in Fig. 8c and d, the changes in PL intensity of CDs and CDs/PMS systems were dependent on pH values. For X-CDs/PMS system, the PL intensity showed 26.60%, 34.24% and 47.19% decrease with the increased pH value, while the C-CDs/PMS system exhibited a relatively large reduction rate with value of 60.14%, 73.56% and 69.38% at pH 5, 7, 9, respectively. The decrease of PL intensity indicated the consumption of e− by PMS, and the decrease ratios were in good line with degradation activity of CDs/PMS system under different pH values. Such difference between X-CDs/PMS system and C-CDs/PMS system proved the excellent e− transfer ability of C-CDs benefiting from the doping effect of graphitic N. The results validated the high efficiency of the C-CDs/PMS system maintained over a wide range of pH values, which could be applied to actual wastewater treatments.

As shown in Fig. 9 a, significant degradation of PNP by C-CDs/PMS system can be observed from the decrease of absorption intensity, confirming that the MB removal was attributed to photocatalysis rather than the photosensitization. Furthermore, after five successive runs, photocatalytic degradation of MB by C-CDs/PMS system decreased from 98.04% to 86.83%, proving great stability of C-CDs/PMS system (Fig. 9b). The accumulation of aromatic intermediates was reduced by abundant hydrophilic groups on the C-CDs surface, thus preventing the coverage of surface active sites [57].

Considering that inorganic anions existing in actual wastewater, including HCO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and Cl−, may have significant effects on the progress of catalytic reactions, the influence of these anions on the PMS activation by C-CDs/PMS system for MB degradation under irradiation was investigated (Fig. 9c). Thereinto, the presence of HCO3 − and CO3 2− had adverse effect to some extent on MB degradation in C-CDs/PMS system, leading to the degradation ratio decreased to 90.34% and 87.88%. Such phenomenon could be interpreted by the fact that HCO3 − and CO3 2− can compete with the target contaminants to react with SO4 •− or •OH through Eqn 1 and 2 and Eqn 7 and 8(7–8) [45]. Further, it was also reported that CO3 2− has a quenching effect on •OH [49]. Apart from HCO3 − and CO3 2−, the other inorganic had little inhibition on PMS activity. As shown in Fig. 9d, when the concentration of inorganic anions was raised to 250 mM, the C-CDs/PMS degradation efficiency of MB was weakly affected in comparison with other reported PMS coupling system [58,59]. These results indicated that the as-prepared C-CDs were excellent PMS activator even at a high concentration of inorganic anions.
(7) HCO3 − + SO4 •− → HCO3 • + SO4 2−
(8) CO3 2− + SO4 •− → CO3 •− +SO4 2−

For the past few years, CDs have demonstrated their photocatalytic application possibilities and raised great research interest [13,15,57]. Compared with previous researches, we found that the S-doped CDs with electron-trapping state could inhibit the electron-hole recombination efficiently, thus being advantageous in the photocatalysis. Moreover, the existence of N-related chemical species (such as pyrrolic N and graphitic N) within the nanostructures of S, N-doped CDs can dramatically decrease their catalytic efficiency by electron-donating effect, but such co-doping structures demonstrated their great advantages in PMS activation. In a nutshell, by taking advantage of the tunable surface chemistry of CDs, our research provides a facile strategy for synthesizing CDs-based catalyst or PMS activator.

In this work, the low-cost X-CDs and C-CDs, were facilely synthesized from xylose and chitosan, respectively, with the doping effect of thionyl chloride as identical extra dopant. The almost same surface defect states generating from O- and S-related groups dominated the PL emission behavior of the two types of CDs, but showing different photocatalytic activity. Based on systematical investigation, the CDs with high catalytic properties was highly related to the formation of new energy level that was induced by S-containing groups, because the S-related energy level can facilitate the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs by the strong electron trapping behavior, while the co-doping effect of S and N heteroatoms could weaken the trapping capacity of S-related energy level due to the strong electron donating of N-related functionalities, Although S, N co-doped CDs utilized for pollutant degradation exhibited inferior catalysis, these nanostructures showed more superior performance in the PMS activation owing to the high electron density of surface state. Although this work has revealed that the N-doped CDs is conducive to PMS activation, the contribution of N-related chemical species including graphitic N, pyrrolic N and pyridine N on the PMS activation capacity is still need to be investigated in further researches.
